did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The study used Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) to analyze 3D hyperspectral imaging data for differentiating malignant from benign pigmented and non-pigmented skin tumors. The CNNs utilized spectral, spatial, and skin-surface model data to perform pixel-wise classification and majority voting diagnosis.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The article is titled 'A Pilot Study' and reports original experimental results from a clinical study involving 42 lesions. It details specific methods (3D hyperspectral imaging, CNNs), data collection (Helsinki University Hospital), and quantitative results (sensitivity, specificity, PPV), indicating it is an original research study rather than a review.

title: Differentiating Malignant from Benign Pigmented or Non-Pigmented Skin Tumours—A Pilot Study on 3D Hyperspectral Imaging of Complex Skin Surfaces and Convolutional Neural Networks
brief_summary: This pilot study evaluated the feasibility of differentiating malignant from benign pigmented and non-pigmented skin tumors using 3D hyperspectral imaging and convolutional neural networks (CNNs). Data were collected from 42 histologically confirmed lesions at Helsinki University Hospital using the SICSURFIS Spectral Imager, which captured spectral, spatial, and skin-surface model data. The CNNs performed pixel-wise classification and majority voting diagnosis based on this combined data. The system achieved a sensitivity of 87% and specificity of 93% for pigmented lesions, and 79% sensitivity and 91% specificity for non-pigmented lesions in pixel-wise analysis. Majority voting analysis correctly diagnosed 41 of 42 lesions, demonstrating high diagnostic accuracy. The results indicate that non-invasive hyperspectral imaging with CNN analysis is feasible for distinguishing tumor types on complex skin surfaces.

lesion_types_studied.details: 7 lesions studied; all were superficial spreading type. Included in both pigmented (3-class) and non-pigmented (4-class) analysis groups.
13 lesions studied. Included both combined and junctional nevi. Used as the benign comparator class in the pigmented lesion analysis.
10 lesions studied (8 in Table 1, 10 in text/abstract). Included nodular, superficial, and pigmented subtypes. Used as a malignant class in the non-pigmented lesion analysis.
5 lesions studied. Breslow thickness varied between 1.1 and 5.2 mm. Used as a malignant class in the non-pigmented lesion analysis.
7 lesions studied. Clinically non-pigmented. Used as the benign comparator class in the non-pigmented lesion analysis.

all_task_types.target: Diagnostic categories of skin tumors (Malignant Melanoma, Benign Pigmented Nevus, Basal Cell Carcinoma, Squamous Cell Carcinoma, Intradermal Nevus) and healthy skin.
all_task_types.details: The study performed pixel-wise classification using three-class (pigmented lesions: MM, PN, healthy skin) and four-class (non-pigmented lesions: BCC, SCC, ID, healthy skin) Convolutional Neural Networks. It also performed majority voting analysis to provide a primary diagnosis for each lesion based on the most common classification of its pixels. The task is binary (malignant vs benign) and multiclass (specific tumor types).

datasets_used.name: Helsinki University Hospital (HUS) Clinical Cohort
datasets_used.description: A prospective clinical study cohort recruited from the Dermatology Outpatient Clinic at Helsinki University Hospital in February and March 2020. Patients were recruited unselected due to diagnosed or suspected skin cancer, or for removal of likely benign nevi. Data was collected using the SICSURFIS Spectral Imager (33 wavelengths, 477–891 nm) capturing hyperspectral, spatial, and skin-surface model data. Lesions were excised for histological confirmation.

use.training: Pixel-wise data from 33 patients (42 lesions) was used to train two CNN models: a three-class classifier (MM, PN, healthy skin) and a four-class classifier (BCC, SCC, ID, healthy skin). Training sets consisted of 29,136 pixels (pigmented) and 31,168 pixels (non-pigmented) selected from the middle frames of hyperspectral images.
use.validation: Validation sets consisted of 1,400 pixels (pigmented) and 1,540 pixels (non-pigmented) used to monitor model performance during training.
use.testing: Test sets consisted of 7,000 pixels (pigmented) and 7,700 pixels (non-pigmented) selected from the right side of the sliced hyperspectral images. Whole image classification and majority voting were also performed on the test data.
use.other: Histological analysis served as the ground truth for all lesions.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To present positive prediction values (PPVs) and evaluate classification performance for pigmented lesions in pixel-wise and majority voting analyses.
To calculate sensitivity, specificity, positive predictive values (PPVs), and F1-score for accuracy reports.
To provide a primary diagnosis for each lesion based on the most common classification of its pixels, serving as a post-processing statistical aggregation method.
To balance the training set's windowed pixels for the CNN models.
To augment the training set for the CNN models.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values or significance tests). It reported descriptive performance metrics (sensitivity, specificity, PPV) and confusion matrices for the CNN models.

Helsinki University Hospital Clinical Co.notes: One low-grade dysplastic benign compound nevus was misclassified as melanoma. 12 of 13 nevi and 7 of 7 melanomas were correctly diagnosed.
All 22 non-pigmented lesions were classified correctly by majority voting.

Helsinki University Hospital Clinical Co.experiment: Majority Voting Analysis (Combined Pigmented and Non-Pigmented)
Helsinki University Hospital Clinical Co.notes: The system correctly diagnosed 41 of 42 lesions overall. The single error was a low-grade dysplastic benign compound nevus misclassified as melanoma.

architecture_used.input_size: 30 × 30 pixel windows with 34 channels (33 spectral bands + 1 skin-surface model)

architecture_details.3d_layer: Extracted features from albedo images (spectral data)
architecture_details.2d_layer: Extracted features from skin-surface models (spatial/depth data)
architecture_details.feature_processing: 3D and 2D layer results were flattened and concatenated to serve as input for hidden layers

training_details.implementation: TensorFlow, Scikit-Learn, Scikit-Image, SciPy Python libraries
training_details.hardware: Linux GPU server with 1 × Tesla P100

configurations: Three-class classifier (Malignant Melanoma, Benign Pigmented Nevus, Healthy Skin)
Four-class classifier (Basal Cell Carcinoma, Squamous Cell Carcinoma, Intradermal Nevus, Healthy Skin)

machine_learning_or_deep_learning_models: Three-class CNN classifier (Malignant Melanoma, Benign Pigmented Nevus, Healthy Skin)
Four-class CNN classifier (Basal Cell Carcinoma, Squamous Cell Carcinoma, Intradermal Nevus, Healthy Skin)
GoogLeNet (mentioned in discussion as a baseline/comparison from previous study)
preprocessing_techniques: Raw image pre-processing: Calculation of radiances and white references from VIS and VNIR channels captured under three different light angles to generate six reflectance hyperspectral images.
Combination of light angles: Different light angles combined into three hyperspectral images.
Photometric stereo: Calculation of albedo images and normals to form one hyperspectral cube per lesion.
Skin-surface model generation: Calculation of depth maps (skin-surface models) from the hyperspectral cubes.
Smoothing: Application of smoothing to the depth maps.
Data normalization: Normalization of skin-surface models between 0 and 1, with infinity and NaN values set to 0.
Manual annotation: Physician (V.L.) manually annotated RGB images to mark tumor and healthy skin areas based on histological diagnosis.
Digitization of annotations: Annotations digitized to binary form to create ground truth masks.
Healthy skin mask creation: Creation of healthy skin masks to select healthy skin pixels around the lesion.
Annular boundary algorithm: Creation of an algorithm to provide an annular boundary around lesions to exclude low-quality areas at the periphery.
Margin exclusion: Exclusion of a 60-px-wide margin at the lesion border to reduce bias from annotations.
Frame selection: Selection of the most significant skin-surface frame (575 nm wavelength) based on previous pilot study findings.
Vertical slicing: Vertical slicing of raw hyperspectral images through the middle of the lesions to create training and test images.
Random pixel selection: Random selection of 250 lesion and 100 healthy skin pixels for each training and test image.
Windowing: Selection of middle pixels from 30 × 30 pixel windows with 34 channels using a rolling-window algorithm.
Class balancing: Balancing of training set's windowed pixels using random over-sampling via the imbalanced-learn library.
Data augmentation: Augmentation of the training set with vertical and horizontal flipping.

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Applied to the training set's windowed pixels for both the three-class (pigmented lesions) and four-class (non-pigmented lesions) CNN models to augment the data.
Used via the imbalanced-learn library to balance the training set's windowed pixels, addressing class imbalance in the dataset.

conclusions: The study concludes that non-invasive hyperspectral imaging combined with convolutional neural network (CNN) analysis is feasible for differentiating between malignant and benign pigmented and non-pigmented skin tumors, even on complex skin surfaces.
The system achieved high diagnostic accuracy, with majority voting analysis correctly diagnosing 41 of 42 lesions overall.
For pigmented lesions, the system demonstrated a weighted sensitivity of 95% and specificity of 97% using majority voting analysis.
For non-pigmented lesions, the system achieved 100% weighted sensitivity, specificity, and positive predictive value (PPV) using majority voting analysis.
The inclusion of shape and depth data (skin-surface models) alongside spectral data via photometric stereo improved classification capabilities compared to previous hyperspectral imaging studies.
The single misclassification involved a low-grade dysplastic benign compound nevus being classified as melanoma, highlighting the challenge of distinguishing benign nevi from melanomas.
important_remarks: The study is a pilot study with a very small sample size (42 lesions from 33 patients), limiting statistical power and generalizability.
Data was collected from a single center (Helsinki University Hospital), introducing potential population and dataset bias; external validation is lacking.
The dataset is highly imbalanced, with only 5 Squamous Cell Carcinoma (SCC) lesions and 7 Intradermal Nevi, which may affect model robustness for these classes.
A 60-pixel margin was excluded at the lesion border to reduce annotation bias, potentially discarding diagnostically relevant edge information.
The study excluded 6 lesions due to imaging artifacts or histological discrepancies, which may introduce selection bias.
The single misclassification involved a low-grade dysplastic benign compound nevus being classified as melanoma, highlighting the difficulty in distinguishing benign nevi from melanomas.

The CNN models were trained on pixel-wise data derived from whole images, raising potential concerns about data leakage if pixel windows overlap significantly between training and test sets, although the paper describes slicing the image into left (training) and right (test) halves.
The study is part of a three-phase pilot study; results should be interpreted as preliminary feasibility evidence rather than definitive clinical validation.
No formal statistical hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values) was reported; results are presented as descriptive metrics (sensitivity, specificity, PPV).
